pattern: The running maximum from the left and the running minimum from the right at each index are exactly the two halves of the instability score, so precomputing both in a single left-to-right and right-to-left pass turns a scan of quadratic recomputation into one linear sweep.
hints: The instability score at index i only ever needs two numbers: the largest value seen so far up to i, and the smallest value still to come from i onward.
Precompute a prefix-maximum array and a suffix-minimum array in two linear passes, then the score at any index is just prefix_max[i] - suffix_min[i].
Scan the precomputed scores from index 0 upward and return the first index whose score is at most k; if none qualifies, the answer is -1.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, first stable index is at position 3
statement example, no index is ever stable
single-element array, k as large as possible
strictly increasing array, index 0 is always stable regardless of k
strictly decreasing array: the running suffix minimum is the same final element at every index, so the score is flat and the answer hinges entirely on k
all values equal, so every index is stable and the first one wins
k exactly meets the minimum achievable instability score, off-by-one on the boundary

stress.description: random small arrays with random k, matching the length-100 constraint for this variant
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, 1000000000)
    return [nums, k]
